subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — Taylor Swift, Voter Registration, and Youth Turnout
stimulus:
In October of 2018, musician Taylor Swift used the social media site Instagram to endorse candidates for the United States Congress in the upcoming election. The post was a rare political statement from the pop star, who noted that "I've been reluctant to publicly voice my political opinions, but I feel very differently about that now. I always have and always will cast my vote based on which candidate will protect and fight for the human rights I believe we all deserve in this country.... So many intelligent, thoughtful, self-possessed people have turned 18 in the past two years and now have the right and privilege to make their vote count. But first you need to register, which is quick and easy to do."

Immediately following Swift's Instagram post, thousands of 18–24 year olds signed up to vote. Swift added a link to the website vote.org in her bio, which lists voting procedures in various states.

parts.prompt: Referencing the scenario, describe the structural barrier to voting that is discussed above.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must identify voter registration as the structural barrier referenced in the scenario. Accept any description that correctly identifies registration requirements as the barrier.

scoring_points.description: Referencing the scenario, describe the structural barrier to voting that is discussed above.

required_elements: Taylor Swift's Instagram post addresses voter registration procedures which are a structural barrier to voting.
alternatives.correct_example: Taylor Swift's Instagram post addresses voter registration procedures which are a structural barrier to voting.

wrong_examples: Lack of political knowledge is a barrier. (Not supported by the scenario — the scenario specifically discusses registration)
Young people don't care about voting. (Identifies a motivational issue, not a structural barrier — and contradicts the scenario)
common_traps: The prompt says 'referencing the scenario' — the answer must be drawn directly from the text; voter registration is explicitly named in the scenario

A structural barrier is a legal or procedural obstacle built into the election system — voter registration is the classic AP Gov example; do not confuse with attitudinal barriers like low political efficacy

parts.prompt: Explain why the structural barrier described in part A may be more of an obstacle in some states than in others.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect variation in the structural barrier to federalism or state-controlled election rules. Generic statements about states being different do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Explain why the structural barrier described in part A may be more of an obstacle in some states than in others.

required_elements: The states get to determine rules for elections. Due to federalism, registration procedures vary from state to state.
alternatives.correct_example: The states get to determine rules for elections. Due to federalism, registration procedures vary from state to state.

required_elements: Legislators and citizens in different states have different views on what registration procedures should be in place.
alternatives.correct_example: Legislators and citizens in different states have different views on what registration procedures should be in place.

wrong_examples: Some states have more resources. (Does not explain the constitutional reason why registration rules vary — does not reference federalism or state authority over elections)
Some states are more politically engaged than others. (Describes a cultural difference, not a structural explanation rooted in federalism)
common_traps: The key concept here is federalism — states have authority over their own election administration, which leads to variation in registration laws (e.g., same-day registration vs. 30-day prior deadlines)
Simply saying 'states are different' without explaining the constitutional basis (federalism, Tenth Amendment, state authority over elections) does not earn the point

parts.prompt: If youth voter turnout increases as a result of the actions in the scenario, explain how this could affect the way candidates run campaigns in the future.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific change in campaigning strategy driven by increased youth voter turnout. Accept any plausible campaign adjustment that addresses the youth demographic.

scoring_points.description: If youth voter turnout increases as a result of the actions in the scenario, explain how this could affect the way candidates run campaigns in the future.

required_elements: Candidates might recruit celebrities to campaign for them on social media.
alternatives.correct_example: Candidates might recruit celebrities to campaign for them on social media.

required_elements: Candidates are likely to engage their supporters on social media to register, vote, donate money, and/or attend campaign events.
alternatives.correct_example: Candidates are likely to engage their supporters on social media to register, vote, donate money, and/or attend campaign events.

required_elements: Candidates may adopt and focus on issues that are more important to younger voters.
alternatives.correct_example: Candidates may adopt and focus on issues that are more important to younger voters.

wrong_examples: Candidates will get more votes. (Does not explain how campaign strategy would change — just states an outcome)
Candidates will try to win young people's votes. (Too vague — must describe a specific strategic change such as increased social media use, celebrity endorsements, or policy focus)
common_traps: The question asks how campaigns would change, not what the election outcome would be — focus on specific strategy adaptations
The explanation must be connected to the scenario: because more young people are voting, campaigns would adapt to target them specifically
